did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a mixed re-sampled (MRS) class-imbalanced semi-supervised learning method for skin lesion classification. The method employs Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically ResNeXt-101-32x8d as the backbone) along with data augmentation techniques like Mixup and Fmix to classify skin lesions from the ISIC skin 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel 'mixed re-sampled (MRS) class-imbalanced semi-supervised learning method' for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology involving re-sampling and data augmentation (Mixup, Fmix), describes experimental implementation on the ISIC skin 2019 dataset using a ResNeXt-101-32x8d backbone, and presents original quantitative results comparing the proposed method against baselines like MeanTeacher and ICT. The structure includes Introduction, Methodology, Experiments, and Conclusion, indicating original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: Mixed Re-Sampled Class-Imbalanced Semi-Supervised Learning for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin lesion classification by proposing a mixed re-sampled (MRS) class-imbalanced semi-supervised learning method. The approach utilizes the ResNeXt-101-32x8d backbone on the ISIC skin 2019 dataset, employing re-sampling strategies and data augmentation techniques like Mixup and Fmix to leverage unlabeled data. The model was evaluated using metrics including AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and normalized multi-class accuracy (NMCA) against baselines such as MeanTeacher, ICT, and MixMatch. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed MRS method achieved state-of-the-art performance, notably securing the highest overall AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and NMCA among the compared semi-supervised methods. The findings confirm the effectiveness of the MRS method in improving classification performance on class-imbalanced datasets with limited labeled data.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the ISIC skin 2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for 8 classes. Evaluation metrics include AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and normalized multi-class accuracy (NMCA), which are standard for classification tasks. The method addresses class imbalance in this classification setting.

datasets_used.name: ISIC skin 2019 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public skin dermoscopy image dataset used for skin lesion classification. The study utilized a subset of the training data, splitting it into labeled, unlabeled, and validation sets, and evaluated the final model on a held-out test set.

use.training: Used for semi-supervised learning. The training set (25,331 images) was split into labeled data (2,000 NV, 800 MEL, 400 BCC, 400 BKL, 200 AK, 200 SCC, 100 VASC, 100 DF) and unlabeled data. The model was trained for 200 epochs using the proposed MRS method.
use.validation: Used to verify the effectiveness of the method. A validation set of 800 images (100 from each of the 8 classes) was created from the training set.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model performance. A test set of 8,238 images was used, for which public labels were not available, but performance metrics were reported.
use.other: The study also compared results against top-ranking records from the ISIC-2019 skin lesion classification challenge leaderboard.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.method: Oversampling of minor class labeled data
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure fair comparison and good generalization performance on class-imbalanced distribution for baseline methods (MeanTeacher, ICT, MixMatch).
To address class imbalance by ensuring uniformly distributed input label samples at the beginning of training and gradually increasing the proportion of majority classes as training progresses.
To quantitatively evaluate performance, defined as the average recall score weighted by category prevalence, serving as the gold indicator for ranking in the ISIC 2019 challenge.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantitatively evaluate classification performance, defined as the probability that a positive instance has a higher confidence score than a negative instance.
To quantitatively evaluate classification performance using True Positives (TP), False Negatives (FN), True Negatives (TN), and False Positives (FP).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using quantitative metrics (AUC, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, NMCA) without statistical significance testing.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_details.modification: The last 1000-dimensional fully connected (FC) layer was replaced with an 8-dimensional FC layer for 8-class classification

training_details.learning_rate: Start learning rate of 10^-5, divided by 5 every 50 epochs
training_details.regularization: Weight decay of 0.02 at each update
training_details.ema: Exponential moving average of parameters with decay rate of 0.999
training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy loss combined with Focal Loss (implied by 'focal loss is introduced' and 'RMS without focal loss' ablation), and unsupervised consistency loss

hyperparameters.unsupervised_loss_weight_U1_U2: Increased from 0 to 1 in the first 16 epochs

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: RMS (Mixed Re-Sampled class-imbalanced semi-supervised learning method)
preprocessing_techniques: RandAugment data augmentation strategy applied to images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to labeled and unlabeled images before cropping. It is a variant of AutoAugment that does not require learning an augmentation strategy from labeled data. Used to mitigate overfitting of minor classes.
Applied after RandAugment to improve the augmentation effect.

Used to mix labeled data with pseudo-labeled unlabeled data. Samples are generated using a Beta distribution with hyperparameter alpha (a=0.75). The mixing coefficient lambda is defined as max(lambda, 1-lambda). Half of the mixed labeled samples and half of the mixed unlabeled samples are collected for loss calculation.

Used to mix the other half of labeled and pseudo-labeled unlabeled data. Involves sampling a random complex tensor, scaling components via decay power, performing an inverse Fourier transform, and creating a binary mask M. The ratio of '1's in the mask (lambda'') is ensured to be > 0.5; if < 0.5, the mask is inverted and lambda'' becomes 1-lambda''.
Applied to pseudo-labels of unlabeled data to sharpen the distribution. Defined as p_i^T / sum(p_j^T). Temperature parameter T is set to 0.5.

A sampling strategy rather than a geometric transform, but used to balance class distribution during training. At epochs 0, 80, 120, and 160, the ratio of major to minor class labeled data in a batch is adjusted to 1:1, 3:1, 5:1, and 1:1 respectively, ensuring uniformly distributed input label samples initially and gradually increasing the proportion of majority classes.

conclusions: The proposed Mixed Re-Sampled (MRS) class-imbalanced semi-supervised learning method effectively addresses the challenge of class imbalance in skin lesion classification by utilizing unlabeled data and re-sampling strategies.

The MRS method achieved state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC skin 2019 dataset, securing the highest overall AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and Normalized Multi-class Accuracy (NMCA) compared to baseline semi-supervised methods (MeanTeacher, ICT, MixMatch) and supervised learning.

The integration of re-sampling ensures uniformly distributed input label samples at the beginning of training and gradually increases the proportion of majority classes, which improves the model's ability to learn general knowledge across the data distribution.
Using Mixup and Fmix to mix labeled data with pseudo-labeled unlabeled data further improves the generalization performance of the semi-supervised learning model.

The proposed method demonstrates better generalization capability than baseline methods, particularly in scenarios where labeled and unlabeled data are unevenly distributed, a limitation where standard semi-supervised methods like MixMatch and MeanTeacher often degrade in performance.
The MRS method is effective for the realistic clinical scenario of skin lesion classification where high-quality annotations are scarce and class distributions are imbalanced.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC skin 2019 dataset, which has a heavily imbalanced class distribution (e.g., Vascular Lesion has only 253 total samples vs. 12,875 for Melanocytic Nevus), reflecting real-world clinical scarcity but limiting generalizability to other populations.

The test set (8,238 images) lacks public labels; performance was evaluated using the model's own predictions, which prevents independent verification of absolute accuracy and introduces potential self-verification bias.

Baseline semi-supervised methods (MeanTeacher, ICT, MixMatch) were artificially balanced by oversampling minor class labeled data to ensure fair comparison, which may overestimate their performance in true class-imbalanced scenarios.

The proposed method achieved state-of-the-art results on the ISIC 2019 leaderboard comparison, but this involved an ensemble of three models trained on different subsets of labeled data, which may not reflect the performance of a single model deployment.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed MRS method and baselines.
The model was trained on a single dataset (ISIC 2019) without external validation on independent dermoscopy datasets, raising concerns about external validity and potential dataset-specific biases.

The method assumes that unlabeled data distribution is unknown to the model during training, but the validation set was used for model selection, which could introduce selection bias if the validation set is not representative of the test distribution.
